segments.s671-g65: بشهر اندر آمد is a poetic inversion: اندر آمد (entered/ came in) is the verb phrase and بشهر specifies the place (into the city). زنخچیرگاه is a compound noun 'place of chains/prison' functioning as the subject/thing that arrives; in poetry the verb may precede or stand between locative and subject.
segments.s671-g66: ازان کار = 'from that deed / because of that action' (a causal locution). بگشاد is the past (simple) of گشودن 'to open' used here as 'opened'. لب برسپاه = 'his lip/mouth toward the army' (لب 'lip/mouth' + برسپاه 'toward the host'), so the clause means 'he opened his mouth toward the army'—a speech-introducing action.
segments.s671-g67: نگه کرد is a two-word verbal phrase meaning 'cast a look / looked' (نگه 'gaze' + کرد auxiliary). Here the proper name/epithet that follows (خراد برزین بدوی) functions as the subject: classical/poetic Persian often places the verb before its subject for emphasis, so the whole chunk reads 'Khurad Barzin Badavi looked.'
segments.s671-g68: چنین گفت is a speech formula 'thus said' (چنین 'thus' + گفت 'said'). کای is a vocative 'O', followed by مهتر 'chief/elder' as the addressee. راست گوی is a descriptive compound: راست 'true' + گوی (agent-noun from گفتن) = 'truth-speaker' or 'one who speaks truly.' The phrase introduces direct address.
segments.s671-g69: بنخچیرگاه = 'in the chain-place/prison' (بن- prefix + noun), این شگفتی = 'this marvel/wonder' (این 'this' + شگفتی 'marvel'), چه بود = 'what was' (چه 'what' + بود past of 'to be'). The clause asks 'what was this wonder in the chain-place?' with normal SOV elements compressed for poetic effect.
segments.s671-g70: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'that/which': آنکس ندید و نه هرگز شنود = 'that no one saw and nor ever heard.' آنکس 'that person/anyone', ندید negative past of دیدن 'did not see', و linking 'and', هرگز 'ever/never', شنود here functions as 'heard'—classical poetic past form.
segments.s671-g71: ورا is a dative/locative 'to him' or 'toward him'. پهلوان is the subject 'the champion/hero', هیچ پاسخ = 'no answer' (هیچ 'no/none' + پاسخ 'reply'), and نداد is the negative past of دادن 'gave' (here 'did not give'). The clause means 'the hero gave him no answer.'
segments.s671-g72: دژم بود = 'he was sullen/grim' (دژم adjective + بود past copula). سر سوی ایوان نهاد is a compound motion: سر 'head' + سوی 'toward' + ایوان 'iwan/porch' + نهاد past of نهادن 'placed/put'—here idiomatically 'he bowed/turned his head toward the iwan' (poetic way to say he lowered or inclined his head).
segments.s671-g401: که is a relative/connective 'who/that' introducing this subordinate clause. برآسمان is a prepositional phrase 'on high / in the sky' placed before its object (اختران). اختران is the direct object 'stars' (here the ordinary noun); بشمرد is 3rd person singular past (archaic narrative form with the prefix ب-) of شمردن 'to count', placed at the end as the finite verb.
segments.s671-g402: خم is a noun 'curve / bend' that takes the following noun phrase چرخ گردنده as the thing bent (i.e. 'the bend of the revolving wheel'); گردنده is a present participle/adjective 'rotating' modifying چرخ. را is the definite object marker attached to the noun phrase, and بشکرد is a past tense verb (archaic narrative form) meaning 'caused to fall / broke / made collapse' so the whole clause means 'made the bend of the revolving wheel collapse.' Word order: noun phrase + object marker + verb.
segments.s671-g403: به introduces the state or result 'into / in' (here 'into humiliation and lamentation'). خواری و زاری are coordinated nouns 'humiliation and lamentation.' بساری is an adverb 'greatly / abundantly' modifying the verb, and فتاد is the past tense 'fell' used idiomatically with به to mean 'fell into (a state).' Word order: prepositional phrase + adverb + verb.
segments.s671-g404: از is a causal/prepositional 'from / because of.' اندیشه is 'thought,' with the adjective کژ 'perverse / wrong' following it (classical Persian commonly places adjectives after nouns). وز here links the following descriptor (functioning like 'and' / 'with' in context) to add another cause, and بدنهاد is an adjective 'ill‑natured / ill‑born' describing disposition; together the phrase supplies the cause ('because of perverse thought and an ill‑natured temperament').
segments.s671-g169: کنون is a fronted adverb meaning "now." چاره‌ای کن is an imperative construction: چاره (a solution, with the indefinite -ای) is the object and کن is the 2nd-person singular imperative "do/make." The following که تا sequence introduces a subordinate (purpose/result) clause; آن سپاه is the subject of that clause (that army).
segments.s671-g170: ز marks origin/place here (poetic preposition = 'from') and governs بلخ. آوری is the 2nd-person present/subjunctive (optative-like) form of آوردن used after a purpose marker ("so that you bring"). سوی این بارگاه is a prepositional phrase 'toward/to this palace/court' with سوی preceding the noun.
segments.s671-g171: پشیمان شد is a simple past predicate 'became repentant/regretful.' از introduces the thing lamented or regretted (here in the sense 'about/regarding'). دوکدان شهریار is a noun phrase in which شهریار (the king) is linked to دوکدان (spinning-house/shop); the whole phrase is the object of از.
segments.s671-g172: وزان is a fused conjunctional phrase (و + ز + آن) meaning 'and from that / and about that,' linking back to the preceding از-clause. پنبه و جامه are coordinated nouns ('cotton and garment'), and نابکار is a postpositive adjective modifying جامه ('wicked/ill-made garment').
segments.s671-g173: برین بر is an archaic/poetic locative phrase meaning 'on/at this' (بر + این, spelled برین); نیامد is past negative 'did not come/occur.' بسی روزگار is an adverbial phrase 'many a time / many days' modifying the verb phrase. Overall: 'Many days did not come upon this' → 'many days passed without...'.
segments.s671-g174: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that / such that') giving the missing event: آمد کس is 'anyone came' (آمد = came, کس = someone/anyone). از پهلوان سوار uses از to mark class/source: 'a man from the champions, a mounted champion' — i.e. the clause says no one came who was a mounted champion.
segments.s671-g175: یکی is an indefinite subject 'one (a certain one).' سله is a noun here (contextually a pouch/saddle-bag/stock; listed sense 'gift/recompense' is the lexeme's primary gloss but contextually a receptacle) and is modified by the compound adjective پرخنجری ('پر-' prefix meaning 'full of' + خنجر 'dagger') = 'full of daggers.' داشته is a past participle/past perfective 'had/possessed.'
segments.s671-g176: یکایک is an adverb 'one by one / each in turn.' سرتیغ is a bound noun 'tip/point of the blade' or 'spearhead' and is the object of برگاشته, which is a perfect/past form meaning 'has lifted/raised' (بر- + گاشته, here 'raised/set up'). The sequence describes a repeated action of lifting blade-tips one by one.
segments.s671-g249: نیرزید is a past negative verb (archaic form) meaning ‘did not tremble/was not shaken’; it appears before the subject in this poetic word order. ایران is the subject ('Iran/the land of Iran'). The phrase بیک مهره موم follows as a minimal object/phrase describing the thing before which Iran did not tremble: مهره موم = 'a wax piece/pawn' (مهره here as a game-piece/ pawn) and بیک functions as ‘one/a single’ (poetic contraction), so the whole chunk means 'for a single wax pawn'.
segments.s671-g250: وزان پس is a temporal/consecutive phrase: وزان = 'and/from that (time)' and پس = 'then/after that'. همی‌داشت is the progressive/imperfective construction (همی + داشت) meaning 'kept/was holding' or 'continued to have'; آهنگ here means 'intention/aim', and روم is the target 'Rome/Byzantium'. The clause therefore states a continuing intention directed at Rome.
segments.s671-g251: بپرموده is a past participial/adjectival form meaning 'well instructed' (modifying the following noun). The conjunction و links the participial phrase to the main clause. ساوه شاه آن رسید places the core narrative: 'the king reached (arrived at) Saveh' (آن here is demonstrative/time adverbial 'there/then' modifying رسید). The verb رسید is simple past 'arrived/attained.'
segments.s671-g252: که introduces a relative/explanatory clause 'that/which': کس ... ندید = 'no one saw' (کس here functions as 'no one' in combination with the negative verb ندید). درجهان is a fused prepositional phrase (در جهان) meaning 'in the world' (here presented as a single token). آن شگفتی = 'that marvel/wonder' (آن as emphatic demonstrative), and ندید is past negative of 'to see' — so the clause says that such a marvel had not been seen by anyone in the world.
segments.s671-g253: اگر چه is a concessive conjunction 'although/though'. فراوان is an adverb 'greatly/much'. کشیدیم is the past tense first-person plural of کشیدن used here in the sense 'to endure/suffer' (we endured), and رنج is the noun 'suffering/hardship' governed by کشیدیم. The clause therefore means 'Although we suffered much.'
segments.s671-g254: This is a correlative negative construction: نه ... نه ... = 'neither ... nor ...'. شان here means 'dignity/status/standing' (or 'stature'); پیل is 'elephant' (used figuratively for might/size). ماندیم is past 'we remained/ were left' (here negative: 'did not remain to us'). زان پس = 'after that' (زان = 'from that', پس = 'then/after'). گنج is 'treasure/wealth'. Together: 'neither the dignity (might) of an elephant remained, nor afterwards any treasure.'
segments.s671-g255: بنوی here functions as a narrative formula ('it is written' / 'one reads' / imperative-like narration; the form can be read as a formulaic introducer). یکی گنج بنهاد شاه is the main clause: یکی گنج = 'one/a single treasure', بنهاد is past 'placed/established/founded', and شاه is the subject 'the king'. Word order is verb-final for the verbal phrase بنهاد شاه, but the narrative introducer precedes them.
segments.s671-g256: توانگر شد is a simple past-formation meaning 'he became wealthy' (توانگر = wealthy; شد = became). آشفته شد repeats the same copular pattern to mean 'he became agitated/disturbed' (آشفته = disturbed; شد = became). بر سپاه is a prepositional phrase 'against/toward the army' or 'concerning the army' that attaches to آشفته شد, indicating the object/target of his agitation.
segments.s671-g273: This is a temporal clause: 'برادر' (subject) + 'چو' (conjunction 'when') + 'آواز خواهر' (possessive noun phrase 'the sister's voice') + 'شنید' (past tense, 3rd sg. of شنیدن 'to hear'). Word order is typical poetic Persian where the temporal 'چو' precedes the event; 'آواز خواهر' is a genitive/possessive construction ('voice of the sister').
segments.s671-g274: 'زگفتار وپاسخ' = 'from/by the words and the reply' (ز = preposition 'from/by', coordinating و joins گفتار and پاسخ). 'فرو آرمید' is a compound where فرو (adverbial/prefix meaning 'deeply/utterly') + آرمید (past of آرامیدن 'to be calm/rest') yields 'was deeply calmed/was hushed' — a poetic way to say 'fell silent.'
segments.s671-g275: چنان هم = 'thus too/so as well' (چنان adverb 'thus', هم 'also/together'); زگفتار ایرانیان = 'by/through the speech of the Iranians' (ز = 'from/by', ایرانیان = subject/agent phrase). The phrase links this state to what was said by the Iranians.
segments.s671-g276: بماندند is past plural 'they remained/were left'. یکسر is an adverb 'entirely/altogether' modifying the verb. زبیم is the prepositional phrase 'ز بیم' = 'from/from fear (i.e. because of fear)' and زیان is the noun 'loss/harm' — together 'completely remained from fear of loss' i.e. 'were all held back by fear of loss.'
segments.s671-g277: چنین گفت = 'thus he said/spoke' (چنین adverb + گفت, past of گفتن). پس is temporal/adverbial 'then/after that'. گردیه is an archaic/poetic form of گردیدن (past 'he turned') and takes the subject understood from context; با سپاه is a prepositional phrase 'with the host/army'.
segments.s671-g278: که‌ای here introduces direct address; ای is the vocative particle 'O' (the combined orthography 'که‌ای' appears in the manuscript), and it calls out to the addressees. نامداران جوینده راه is a nominal phrase: نامداران 'renowned/illustrious' modifying جوینده (participle/noun 'seekers') with راه 'the way/path' as the object of seeking — 'O famed seekers of the way.'
segments.s671-g279: زگفتار = 'from/by the words' (ز = 'from/at' indicating the cause or reference). خامش is adjective/noun 'silent/silence' placed after the prepositional phrase for emphasis. چرا is the interrogative 'why' and ماندید is past plural 'you remained' — altogether 'Why did you remain silent at these words?'.
segments.s671-g280: چنین 'thus/so' links the consequence; از is the causal 'from/because of'; جگر here is used figuratively as 'heart/seat of feeling' rather than the literal liver; خون is 'blood'; برافشاندید is past plural 'you caused to scatter/sprinkle/stream' (برافشاندن). The clause expresses the consequence: 'thus you have caused blood to gush from your hearts' (i.e. you have made your hearts bleed).
segments.s671-g361: بران داوری is a prepositional phrase 'on/upon that judgment' (بران = 'on that' + داوری = 'judgment'). نگشاد لب is verb + object with the negative prefix ن-: نگشاد = 'did not open' (past) and لب = 'lip(s)'; the subject is omitted (third person). هیچ intensifies the negation ('not at all/no one').
segments.s671-g362: This adverbial phrase sets a time/span: زبرگشتن is 'the (re)turning/return' (z- prefix + برگشتن), followed by the name/term هور; تا نیم شب = 'until midnight' (تا = until; نیم شب = half of the night / midnight). In context it says that Hoor did not return before that hour.
segments.s671-g363: بدو گفت بهرام = 'to him said Bahram' — the indirect object (بدو = 'to him') precedes the verb گفت ('said') and the speaker's name follows, a common poetic order. کای is a vocative particle 'O' addressing the addressee; پاک تن is an epithet 'pure-bodied' (پاک modifying تن).
segments.s671-g364: چه بینی is a direct question: چه = 'what' and بینی is the 2nd‑person present/future form 'you see' / 'do you think'. به گفتار این انجمن is a prepositional phrase 'regarding the speech/words of this assembly' (به = about/regarding; گفتار = speech; این انجمن = this assembly).
segments.s671-g365: ورا گردیه = 'he turned to him' (ورا = 'to him'; گردیه is a past participle/past form 'turned/turned toward'). هیچ پاسخ نداد = literal word order 'no reply he gave' — هیچ modifies پاسخ ('no reply') and نداد is past tense 'he did not give / gave not', with the subject omitted.
segments.s671-g366: نه ... بود شاد gives a negative evaluative clause: نه (not) + از رای آن مهتران ('from/of the counsel of those great ones') + بود شاد ('was glad'). از here means 'concerning' or 'at/over' the opinion; مهتران is 'the great/important men' (elders/chiefs); بود is past copula 'was'.
segments.s671-g367: چنین گفت is 'thus he said' (چنین = thus/so; گفت = said). The following noun phrase اوبا دبیر بزرگ names the speaker (Ouba) with title 'dبیر بزرگ' = 'the great secretary/scribe' — subject after verb is common in epic/persuasive speech.
segments.s671-g368: که‌ای here functions as a vocative/exclamatory address ('O!'); مرد بدساز is an insulting appellation 'man, maker of harm / troublemaker' (مرد = man; بدساز = ill‑maker/trouble‑maker). چون پیرگرگ is a simile 'like an old wolf' (چون = like; پیرگرگ = old wolf), completing the direct address.
segments.s671-g201: سپهبد is the subject (a title 'spahbed'). چو here is a temporal conjunction 'when' introducing the subordinate clause. گفتار ایشان is the object/phrase 'their words/speech' (ایشان = 'they/them'), and شنید is a past-tense verb 'heard' (simple past). Word order is subject + temporal marker + object + verb, typical of older Persian narrative.
segments.s671-g202: دل لشکر is a genitive phrase 'the heart/spirit of the army' (classical Persian omits the ezafe). از introduces cause 'because of / from', تاجور names the group causing the trouble 'the merchants/traders', خسته is an adjective 'weary/tired', and دید is the past-tense verb 'he saw' — so literally 'he saw the army's heart weary because of the merchants.'
segments.s671-g203: بلشکر is an adverbial/phrase meaning 'to/among the army' (archaic fused form). چنین گفت is a fixed phrase 'said thus' that introduces reported speech; گفت here is the past reporting verb. پس functions as a discourse/temporal particle 'then' (or a linker), and پهلوان is the addressee 'champions' (vocative).
segments.s671-g204: که here introduces the content of what was said (subordinate clause). بیدار باشید is a second-person plural present/subjunctive used as an imperative 'be alert'; باشید is the subjunctive/imperative form of بودن. و simply coordinates, and روشن‌روان is a predicate adjective 'clear-/bright-souled, cheerful' describing the addressees — no copula necessary in Persian.
segments.s671-g205: که again introduces a subordinate clause here. خراد برزین is a personal name (compound: خراد as adjective/noun + برزین as name); in older Persian names often appear without linking particles. برشهریار is an archaic prepositional phrase 'to the king' (بر = 'to/onto' in classical usage).
segments.s671-g206: سخنهای (سخن‌های) is the plural object 'the words/speeches', پوشیده is the adjective 'hidden/secret' modifying سخنهای, and کردآشکار is a fused poetic compound of کرد + آشکار meaning 'made manifest/revealed' (active past). The order is object (suffixed plural) + adjective + verb, consistent with SOV tendencies in classical Persian poetry.
segments.s671-g207: کنون is an adverb 'now'. یک بیک is the direct object 'one bey/chieftain' (یک = one, بیک = bey). چاره جان is a noun phrase 'remedy of (your) life' (the ezafe is omitted in poetry), and کنید is the second-person plural imperative/subjunctive 'do/make' — so the clause means 'now make one bey the remedy of life.'
segments.s671-g208: همه 'everyone' is vocative/emphatic here. بامن 'with me' indicates the party to the covenant. امروز 'today' is temporal. پیمان کنید is the compound 'make a covenant/pledge' with کنید again as the 2pl imperative/subjunctive; the whole clause is an exhortation: 'All of you, pledge yourselves to me today.'
segments.s671-g353: زناآمده is a compound/epithet meaning 'an adulterous woman (has come)' used as a topical subject; بد here qualifies the fear (an adverbial or predicative intensifier 'bad/ill'), and بترسی is a present/subjunctive-form verb 'you fear' (classical infinitival/derivational form with ب-). همی is the archaic/enclitic particle meaning 'still/always' or an emphatic marker; together the clause states that an adulterous woman has come and you (still) fear badly.
segments.s671-g354: زدیهیم is a first-person plural perfect/preterite 'we struck' (or a pl. perfect-stative boasting 'we have smitten'); شاهان is the plural object 'kings'. چه here is an exclamatory/interrogative particle 'what/how' and پرسی is a present/archaic 2nd/3rd-person verbal form 'you ask / (do you) ask'; the final همی is an emphatic particle. The clause is therefore a boast followed by a rhetorical question.
segments.s671-g355: بکن is the imperative/infinitive 'do (make)'; کار is the direct noun 'deed/work'. وکرده is a connective phrase 'and (what is) done' or 'and having done', functioning as a participial follow-up; به یزدان سپار is a prepositional phrase 'entrust to God' where سپار is an imperative/infinitive 'entrust/hand over'. The clause gives a two-part command: act (do the work) and commit the result to God.
segments.s671-g356: بخرما is an older/dialectal form built on بخر 'buy' with a following clitic-like object 'ما' — here rendered 'buy (for) us' (literal reading). چه is an interrogative/exclamatory 'what/why'; یازی (from یازیدن) carries the sense 'burst forth / break out'; چوترسی is a subordinate conditional 'if you fear' (چو + ترسی), and زخار is a noun 'filth/scurf' used figuratively. The clause poses a rhetorical question about buying/obtaining something in the face of an outbreak or filthy danger (poetic, elliptical diction).
segments.s671-g357: تن is the noun 'body'; آسان is the predicate/adverb 'easily'. نگردد is a negative present/stative form 'does not become / does not turn into' (classical negative with ن-), and سرانجمن is a compound noun 'head of the assembly' or 'leader of the council'. Word order is subject (تن) + predicate (آسان نگردد) + complement (سرانجمن): 'The body does not easily become the council's head.'
segments.s671-g358: همه 'everyone' is the subject; بیم جان 'fear for life' is a nominal phrase where بیم (fear/anxiety) takes جان ('life/soul') as its object/genitive; باشد is the subjunctive/optative copula 'let it be' or 'should be'. ورنج تن is a coordinated phrase 'and the pain/suffering of the body' (ورنج = 'and pain', followed by تن 'body'). The clause prescribes that all should be anxious for life rather than for bodily suffering.
segments.s671-g359: زگفتارشان is a prepositional/ablative phrase 'from/because of their speech/words' (ز- prefix + گفتارشان 'their words'). خواهر پهلوان is a nominal apposition 'the champion's sister' (خواهر = sister; پهلوان qualifies her as the hero/champion). The phrase links cause (their words) to the person affected (the sister).
segments.s671-g360: همی‌بود is an imperfect/continuous auxiliary 'was / kept being' (arch. particle همی + بود). پیچان is an adjective/participial 'twisting, agitated' and وتیره is a coordinate 'and dark/gloomy' (و + تیره). روان here means 'spirit/mind' (not simply 'flowing'); together the predicate describes her state of mind as twisted and dark.
segments.s671-g233: ز + ما here gives a cause/agent phrase 'for/from us'; مهتر is the subject 'the elder/chief'. آزرده شد is a passive/perfect-like verbal phrase 'was distressed' (شد used as auxiliary/past); بی‌گناه is a predicate adjective attached to the subject. Word order is causal phrase → subject → verb → adjectival comment, typical of poetic Persian.
segments.s671-g234: چنین 'thus' adverbially links to what follows. سربپیچید is a compound verb 'سر + پیچید' meaning 'turned his head/averted' (past). زآیین وراه is a prepositional phrase 'from/of custom and the road/path' (ز governing both آیین and راه, joined by و). The clause states the resultant action after being distressed.
segments.s671-g235: چه سازید is an elliptical rhetorical question 'what is to be done' (سازید here functions as an impersonal/optative present of ساختن, 'do/arrange'). ودرمان links the question to 'and as for remedy' (ودرمان = 'and in remedy/for a cure'). این کارچیست is a fused interrogative 'what is this matter?'. The word order places the question-marking چه up front, then the verb phrase, then the linked noun phrase.
segments.s671-g236: نباید is an impersonal negative modal 'it must not / it is not right'. که introduces the following clause. برخسته is a past-participial/adjectival form 'having risen / having taken action'. باید is the positive modal 'must/ought' and is followed by گریست here in an older/poetic shape meaning 'to weep / one must weep' — the clause contrasts prohibition of action (نباید) with the necessity to lament (باید گریست).
segments.s671-g237: هرآنکس که introduces a free relative 'whoever / anyone who'. پوشید is past tense 'hid/covered' and here takes درد as its object: 'hid (his) pain'. ازپزشک is a prepositional phrase 'from the physician' specifying from whom the pain was concealed. The clause is a conditional/characterizing clause describing such a person.
segments.s671-g238: زمژگان 'eyelashes/eyes' functions as the source phrase; فروریخت is the past verb 'fell/cascaded down'. خونین is an adjective 'bloody / blood-red' modifying سرشک 'teardrop' (a diminutive 'drop/tear'). Order is poetic: source (from the lashes) + verb + adjectival noun phrase giving the falling objects.
segments.s671-g239: زدانندگان‌گر is a fused prepositional + conditional phrase: ز 'from' + دانندگان 'the knowing/knowing ones' + گر (if) giving 'if (from) the knowers...' — effectively 'if we hide it from those who know'. بپوشیم is 1st-person-plural subjunctive/optative 'we should/may hide/conceal' and راز is its object 'the secret.'
segments.s671-g240: شود is the impersonal/present-future 'becomes / will be'. کارآسان is the predicate 'an easy task'. بما is a dative 'for us'. بر دراز is an adverbial phrase where بر + دراز conveys duration/extent ('for a long time' / 'long-lasting') in this poetic context; together the phrase says the affair will become easy for us (and endure).
segments.s671-g393: نبودند is a past negative copular/existential form meaning 'were not' (past, plural). یازان here functions as an attributive/nominal notion derived from a root meaning 'open' and is attached to بتخت کیان; بتخت is actually the fused prepositional phrase ب + تخت ('on the throne') and کیان is the genitival noun 'the Kians/kings', so the whole chunk says 'there were no openings on the throne of the Kians.'
segments.s671-g394: همه is 'all/everyone' (subject). بندگی را uses را as a definite/emphatic object marker and means 'the servitude' (the noun being taken). کمر here means 'girdle/belt' and برمیان (بر + میان) is a locative phrase 'around the middle / about the waist'; together this idiom means 'girded the belt of servitude' (i.e., they took on servitude).
segments.s671-g395: ببستند is past plural 'they bound/tied' (an action completed by the group). و is the coordinating 'and'. زیشان is composed of the preposition ز (from/of) + ایشان ('them' or 'him/them depending on context) and functions 'from him/them'. بهی is an adjective 'good/excellent' and خواستند is past plural 'they desired/asked/sought' — together 'they sought good from him/them.'
segments.s671-g396: همه دل 'every heart / all hearts' is the noun phrase (subject/affected entity). بفرمانش is a prepositional/agentive phrase 'by his command' (ب + فرمان + -ش). آراستند is past plural active 'they adorned/arrayed' — here it means '(the hearts) were adorned' by his command (agent implied by context), so the verb describes a completed action affecting all hearts.
segments.s671-g397: نه is the negative particle 'not/no'. بیگانه here is adjectival 'foreign/unfamiliar' modifying زیبای افسر; زیبای is a construct form 'beauty/ornament of' and افسر (literary) means 'helmet/crown'. بود is the past copula 'was'. The phrase therefore means 'there was no foreign ornament on the helmet/crown.'
segments.s671-g398: سزای بزرگی is a genitive/construct phrase 'the recompense/desert of greatness' (سزا = recompense, بزرگی = greatness). بگوهر is a predicate adjectival/participle meaning 'set with gems' (be- + گوهر), and بود is the copula 'was' in past: 'the recompense of greatness was (a) jewel.'
segments.s671-g399: زکاوس شاه is a topicalizing phrase 'as for Kāvus the king' (ز here marks the topic/starting point). اندرآیم is a 1st-person singular verbal form 'I enter / I will enter / I take up (the subject)' used by the narrator to introduce the topic. نخست is an adverb 'first/at the outset'. Together: 'As for King Kāvus, I will first take him up.'
segments.s671-g400: کجا is the interrogative 'where'. راه یزدان is a genitive phrase 'the way/path of God' (یزدان = God/divine). همی‌بازجست is a frequentive/past-form verbal compound meaning 'kept seeking / sought persistently' (همی- marks continuous or habitual aspect, بازجست = 'sought'). The segment asks rhetorically 'where did he seek the way of God?'.
segments.s671-g289: همه کارهای شما is a noun phrase 'all/whole of your deeds' (همه + plural noun + possessive شما). ایزدیست is ایزدی + -ست, an adjectival predicate with the old third-person singular copula suffix (equivalent to 'is divine'); in classical Persian adjectives often attach -ست rather than using a separate بودن form.
segments.s671-g290: This string links additional predicates: زمردی functions as an adjective 'emerald-like/greenish' and is coordinated with the following phrases by و. ز is the poetic form of از meaning 'from/by/through', so ز دانش و بخردیست reads 'and (they are) from knowledge and wisdom' with بخردیست = بخرد + -ست ('is/are of wisdom' or 'wise'). The clause uses the same adjectival -ست predicate pattern.
segments.s671-g291: نباید که is an impersonal negative modal construction 'one should not / it is not proper that...'. The following clause uses آوریم (1st-person-plural present-subjunctive/jussive) after که: رای پلنگ آوریم = 'bring/adopt the leopard's counsel' (رای = counsel/plan, پلنگ specifies whose counsel). The verb آوریم expresses taking/adopting here.
segments.s671-g292: The second that-clause explains the content of the leopard's counsel: با هرکسی (prepositional phrase 'with/against anyone') precedes رای جنگ آوریم where رای جنگ 'counsel of war' (رای = plan/counsel, جنگ = war) and آوریم again is the subjunctive 'we adopt/bring about'. The clause as a whole means 'that we decide on war against anyone.'
segments.s671-g293: مجویید is the negative imperative (plural/formal) 'do not seek / do not ask'. ازین پس is the adverbial phrase 'from now on / henceforth' (از + این(=ین) + پس). کس ازمن سخن is the object 'anyone (to) speak/word from me' (کس = anyone, ازمن = from me, سخن = word/speech). Word order places the temporal phrase before the object.
segments.s671-g294: کزین is a fused prepositional marker 'from this / concerning this'; باره‌ام is باره + -ام 'this occasion/matter of mine' (the clitic -am = 'my'). پاسخ آمد is a simple past clause 'an answer came' (subject پاسخ + past آمد). ببن is an imperative 'see/look!' appended to draw attention to the fact.
segments.s671-g295: اگر introduces a conditional 'if'. جنگ سازید uses سازید (2nd-person plural subjunctive/present) with جنگ as object: 'if you make/prep/raise war' (سازیدن used idiomatically 'to make/prepare'). یاری کنیم is the main-clause verb in 1st-person-plural subjunctive/optative ('we will/shall help' or 'we will give aid'), forming the promise/response to the condition.
segments.s671-g296: به پیش سواران is a prepositional phrase 'to/in front of the horsemen' (به + پیش + سواران). سواری کردن is a compound verb (noun + کردن) meaning 'to ride / provide mounted service'; here سواری (noun) + کنیم (1st-plural) yields 'we will ride/ride out' or 'we will provide mounted support'. The verb کنیم is the same auxiliary 'to do' forming the compound.
segments.s671-g369: گمانت is a possessive noun meaning “your thought/belief” (noun + -ت enclitic). چنینست is the copular predicate “it is thus/so,” so the two together mean “your belief is thus.” کین here introduces what is believed (a poetic/deictic linker: “that/this”) and is followed by the two nouns تاج وتخت “crown and throne” as the content of that belief.
segments.s671-g370: سپاه بزرگی and پیروزبخت are a coordinated noun phrase giving the expected consequences: سپاه (army, host) modified by بزرگی (greatness/large) and joined by و to پیروزبخت (fortunate/prosperous). Grammatically these complete the content introduced by کین in the previous segment.
segments.s671-g371: ز گیتی is a prepositional phrase “from/of the world” (poetic ز = از). کسی را is the indefinite object with the postposition را (here marking the person under discussion). نبد (archaic negative existential) means “there was not / does not exist,” and آرزوی is the noun “desire/wish.” The clause therefore asserts the nonexistence of anyone who lacks that wish.
segments.s671-g372: ازان is a deictic/genitive marker “of those/for those,” attaching to نامداران آزاده خوی, an adjectival string: نامداران “the renowned/illustrious,” آزاده “free/noble‑hearted,” and خوی “disposition/character.” Together they describe what is desired: the character or company of those famed, noble‑hearted people.
segments.s671-g373: مگر introduces a counterquestion or challenge here (rhetorical “is it that…”). شاهی is the abstract noun “kingship,” آسانتر is the comparative “easier,” از is the preposition “than/of” and بندگیست is the copula + noun “is servitude.” The whole line asks rhetorically whether being king is really easier than being a servant.
segments.s671-g374: بدین دانش is a prepositional phrase “by/with this knowledge” (بدین = به + این, “with this”), تو is the subject “you,” بباید is the modal “must/ought to,” and گریست is the verb “to weep/laments.” Together: “Because of/with that knowledge you ought to weep.”
segments.s671-g375: بر آیین is a prepositional phrase meaning “according to/on the custom/practice.” شاهان پیشین is a genitive group “the former/ancient kings.” رویم here functions as a hortative/first‑person plural verb form “let us go/let us proceed” or “let us turn (to),” so the segment says “let us proceed according to the custom of earlier kings.”
segments.s671-g376: سخن‌های آن is “the words/speeches of those (aforementioned),” برتران (here ‘the superior/better ones’) modifies آن, and بشنویم is the first‑person plural subjunctive/imperative “let us hear/listen.” The clause therefore urges: “let us hear the words of those higher ones.”
segments.s671-g137: یکایک (adverb) precedes the verb to mean 'one by one'. بگفت is simple past 'he said/told'. آن کجا دیده بود is a subordinate clause: دیده is past participle 'seen' and بود functions as the auxiliary, so دیده بود = 'had seen' (pluperfect); word order places the demonstrative آن before the interrogative کجا ('that/where').
segments.s671-g138: دگر here is an adverb meaning 'other/else' or 'besides'. هرچ‌ازکار is a compound meaning 'whatever concerning the affair'. پرسیده بود is again past participle + بود, a pluperfect 'had asked' with an implicit subject ('he').
segments.s671-g139: ازان is a poetic preposition/adverb 'thereupon/from that'. تاجورماند is a fused form meaning 'the crowned one / he who wore the crown remained' (subject + remaining notion). اندر شگفت places the state 'in wonder' after the verb, yielding 'remained in wonder/astonishment.'
segments.s671-g140: سخن هرچ بشنید = 'whatever speech/words he heard' (هرچ modifies سخن). در دل گرفت is an idiomatic verbal phrase: گرفت (took/seized) with دل as object inside 'در' — 'he took (them) into his heart' or 'it seized his heart'. بشنید is simple past 'he heard'.
segments.s671-g141: چوگفتار موبد functions as a temporal clause head 'when/at the speech of the mobed' (چو here introduces 'when' / 'on the utterance of'). بیاد آمدش is the fixed phrase 'came to his memory' — بیاد 'to memory' + آمدش 'came to him' (with clitic -ش).
segments.s671-g142: ز دل بر یکی سرد باد آمدش is figurative: ز دل 'from the heart', بر یکی 'upon him' (بر = upon), سرد باد آمدش = 'a cold wind came to him' (سرد = cold, باد = wind, آمدش = 'came to him' with clitic). Together it means 'a chill ran through his heart' or 'a cold wind came upon him.'
segments.s671-g143: همان نیز together emphasize 'the very same' or 'just so' (همان = that/the same; نیز = also/indeed). گفتار آن فال‌گوی = 'the utterance/words of that fortune‑teller' — the genitive relationship is expressed by ordering (گفتار + آن فال‌گوی).
segments.s671-g144: که introduces a relative/clause 'which/that'. گفت here stands for the reported utterance (rendered as 'had said' in English). او is the subject 'he'. بپیچد is a present/subjunctive form (prefixed ب-) meaning 'may/should turn' or 'would turn' in reported speech; together او بپیچد = 'that he would turn'. ز تخت تو روی is literal 'his face from your throne' (ز = from, تخت = throne, تو = your, روی = face) i.e. 'turn his face away from your throne.'
segments.s671-g329: 'یکی موبدی داستان زد برین' = 'a certain mobad told this story (thereupon)'. 'یکی' is the indefinite 'a certain'; 'موبدی' names the speaker (a Zoroastrian priest). 'داستان زد' is a poetic collocation where زدن with داستان means 'to tell/utter a tale' (here زدن is used in the sense of 'perform/utter'). 'برین' is composed of بر + این ('upon this' / 'thereupon'), compressed for meter and linking the tale to what precedes.
segments.s671-g330: This is a relative/indefinite clause begun by كه + هرکس كه ('that/whoever'). 'هرکس که' = 'whoever; anyone who'. 'دانا' = 'wise'. The word 'بد' here is an archaic/metrical form standing for 'بود' (was/is) rather than the lexical adjective 'bad'—it shows older poetic morphology. 'وپیش بین' is the compound 'پیش‌بین' (foresighted/one who sees ahead) split for meter; syntactically it follows 'دانا' as a second quality ('wise and foresighted').
segments.s671-g331: اگر introduces a conditional ('if'). 'پادشاهی کند' uses the noun 'پادشاهی' with the verb کردن to mean 'to reign / to exercise kingship' (کند is the present/subjunctive form of کردن). 'یک زمان' is a temporal phrase 'for a while / for a time' modifying the duration of the reign.
segments.s671-g332: 'روانش' = روان + possessive suffix -ش ('his spirit/soul'), the undergoing subject of the verb. 'بپرد' is the subjunctive/optative of پریدن ('to fly, to leap') used here to indicate the soul's ascending as the result of the conditional. 'سوی آسمان' is a prepositional phrase 'toward heaven/sky'.
segments.s671-g333: The phrase begins a comparative preference: 'به ... بودن' functions like 'better to be ...'. 'ازبنده' here denotes 'being a servant' (the lexical list doesn't give a perfect match for this usage, so it's an idiomatic nominal). 'بودن' is the infinitive 'to be'. 'بسال دراز' ('for a long year' / 'for many years') gives the duration — بسال here marks a long period and دراز is the adjective 'long'.
segments.s671-g334: The second half of the comparison: 'به گنج جهاندار بردن نیاز' — 'to take the sovereign's treasure' is the contrasted action. 'به' functions as the comparative marker here ('than'). 'گنج جهاندار' = 'the treasure of the world-ruler/sovereign' (جهاندار = lord/sovereign). 'بردن' is the infinitive 'to take/carry off'. 'نیاز' (here 'need' / 'a state of need') ends the clause, indicating the unfortunate condition tied to that action.
segments.s671-g335: 'چنین گفت' = 'thus he spoke/said' (an idiomatic opening). 'پس' = 'then/thereupon'. The prepositional phrase 'با دبیر بزرگ' follows the verb and marks the addressee ('to/with the great scribe'); in classical order the verb can precede the indirect object phrase.
segments.s671-g336: كه introduces the quoted/command clause. 'بگشای' is the imperative/jussive form meaning 'open' (here used with the object لب 'lip/mouth'); 'را' is the definite object marker (marks لب as the definite object). 'تو‌ای پیرگرگ' is the vocative address 'O you — old wolf' (تو‌ای is emphatic/poetic 'you, O ...', پیرگرگ is a compound epithet 'old wolf').
segments.s671-g313: ازان پس is a two-word adverbial phrase meaning 'after that' or 'thereupon' (here 'thereupon/after that'). The sequence ببهرام بهرام گفت uses a dative/recipient marker به+Name (ببهرام = 'to Bahram') followed by the name as the speaker (بهرام گفت = 'Bahram said'); the verb گفت is past tense, 3rd-person singular, introducing reported speech.
segments.s671-g314: که‌ای here begins the reported speech and functions like a vocative/exclamatory 'O' (classical/poetic usage). با + خردیاروبا literally 'with the wise-companion' addresses the addressee as a wise ally. رای جفت is a compact phrase meaning 'pair/bring your counsel (together)' — رای = 'counsel/plan' and جفت = 'pair/match', so the speaker asks for a joining or matching of counsel.
segments.s671-g315: چه گویی is an interrogative present-tense phrase meaning 'what do you say?' (چه = 'what', گویی here = 'you say' in classical present). کزین is a fused classical particle (که + از این = 'that/from this') introducing a clause 'that from this seeking'. جستن is the infinitive 'to seek', and تخت و گنج are its objects: 'throne and treasure.'
segments.s671-g316: بزرگیست is a predicative nominal 'it is greatness' or 'there is greatness' (بزرگی + است fused as one poetic word). فرجام‌گر is a compound agent-noun 'that which brings an end / finisher'. درد ورنج is a fixed pairing 'pain and toil/suffering' and functions as the complement that the 'فرجام‌گر' terminates.
segments.s671-g317: بخندید is a past, 3rd-person singular verb 'he laughed' (classical word order allows the verb first). بهرام is the subject placed after the verb (common in epic style). ازان داوری is a prepositional phrase 'at/over that judgment' (ازان = 'thereupon / from that', داوری = 'judgement'); together they indicate the target of the laughter.
segments.s671-g318: ازان پس is a temporal adverbial 'thereupon / then'. برانداخت is past tense, transitive, meaning 'he threw / cast (away, up)' — in context 'tossed' — and انگشتری is the direct object 'a ring'. The verb+object form describes the physical act that follows the laughter.
segments.s671-g319: بدو گفت is the lightest, common classical phrase 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him', گفت = 'said'). چندانک introduces a concessive/temporal clause 'so long as / while'. این در هوا = 'this in the air' specifies the condition (here the ring staying aloft).
segments.s671-g320: بماند شود combines two verbs in classical style: بماند (subjunctive/presentive 'remains' / 'while it stays') and شود ('becomes' / 'may become'), yielding 'while this remains ... a servant may become a king.' بنده‌ای is an indefinite noun 'a servant' and پادشا is 'king' (here predicate, what the servant may become).
segments.s671-g377: This is an inverted, somewhat poetic word order: چنين (thus) + داد (gave, past) + پاسخ (answer) form the core ‘gave such an answer’. مر is an archaic/poetic object/indirect-object form meaning “to me” (here functioning as an indirect object), او را is the definite/direct object “him” (را marks it), and دبیر is the subject ‘the scribe’ placed at the end for emphasis.
segments.s671-g378: که‌گر is the conditional particle (که گر = “if”), رای here means a plan/counsel; من is the first-person possessor ‘my’; نیستت is an archaic/poetic present/past copula ‘is not’ with enclitic ending; جایگیر is a predicate meaning ‘settled/has a place’. Together: ‘if my counsel has no place.’
segments.s671-g379: هم آن گوی و آن کن contains two imperatives: گوی (say) and کن (do), with هم and آن intensifying/pointing to ‘those very things’ (هم آن = ‘that very…’). The clause که رای آیدت is a relative/subordinate clause (‘which counsel comes to you’) with که introducing it and آیدت ‘comes to you’ carrying the 2nd-person enclitic.
segments.s671-g380: بران رو is an imperative phrase ‘turn/go toward those (things)’ (بران = ‘toward that/thereon’; رو functions as direction/imperative ‘go/turn’). The following که introduces a purpose/result clause: دل رهنمای آیدت ‘that the heart’s guide may come to you’ — دل ‘heart’, رهنمای ‘guide/advisor’, آیدت ‘comes to you’ (2nd-person enclitic).
segments.s671-g381: همان خواهرش نیز places emphasis/identification: همان ‘that/same’, خواهرش ‘his sister’ (ش = his), نیز ‘also’. بهرام را is the (definite) indirect object marked by را — here Bahram is the person addressed or the one told (word order is poetic; in prose the object normally precedes the verb).
segments.s671-g382: بگفت is the past tense ‘told/said’. آن سواران خودکام را is the definite object phrase ‘those self-willed horsemen’ (آن = those, سواران = horsemen/riders, خودکام = arbitrary/self-willed), with را marking the definite object; the object follows the verb here because of the poem’s word order.
segments.s671-g383: نه نیکوست negates نیکوست (‘is good’ → ‘is not good’). این دانش is the subject ‘this knowledge’. ورای تو is a dative/benefactive phrase ‘for you’ or ‘on you’ (ورای is an older/poetic form indicating direction/benefit and combines with تو ‘you’). The clause reads: ‘This knowledge is not good for you.’
segments.s671-g384: This line gives the consequence: بکژی is a poetic verb meaning ‘go awry/err’ or ‘become perverse’, خرامد is a present/past-form meaning ‘struts/strides proudly’, همی is an adverb (indeed/still), and پای تو ‘your foot/step’. Together they warn that if one adopts that learning one’s step will go astray and one will swagger (i.e., be led into proud or wrong conduct).
segments.s671-g193: 'شکسته' is a predicative adjective meaning 'broken' placed before the noun in poetic word order; 'دگر باره' ('دگر' + 'باره') is a poetic adverbial compound meaning 'once again' (literally 'another time'); 'خنجر' is the noun 'dagger'; 'بود' is the past copula 'was' that completes the clause. The whole clause = 'The dagger was broken again.'
segments.s671-g194: This clause begins with the poetic preposition 'ز' ('from/because of') repeated for emphasis: 'ز زخم و ز دشنام' = 'from wounds and from insults.' 'بتر' is the comparative adjective 'worse.' 'بود' again is the past copula 'was.' The literal order is emphatic (prepositional phrase first) and yields 'it was worse because of wounds and insults.'
segments.s671-g195: 'چنین' ('such') modifies the compound 'شاه برگاه' ('king's court' / 'royal court'). 'هرگز' is the adverb 'never.' 'مباد' is an optative/negative particle meaning 'may it not be' (a fixed archaic/formal wish construction). Taken together: 'May there never be such a royal court.'
segments.s671-g196: This is a continuation with a negative sense: 'نه' (not). 'آنکس' = 'that person / anyone.' The relative particle 'که' introduces the clause 'who takes/claims (گیرد)' — 'گیرد' here is present (or general) 'takes/claims.' 'ازو' = 'from him/of him.' 'نیز' = 'also/too' (here emphatic). 'یاد' = 'remembrance/tribute.' The clause therefore means roughly 'nor the man who would take/claim any remembrance from him.'
segments.s671-g197: 'اگر' is the conditional 'if.' 'نیز' adds 'even/also' as emphasis. 'بهرام پورگشسب' is the proper name 'Bahram, son of Gushasp' (a compound name/patronymic acting as the subject of the conditional). This segment sets up the protasis ('if ...').
segments.s671-g198: 'بران' is a poetic locative/adverbial 'upon that/thereon.' 'خاک درگاه' = 'the dust of the threshold' (inverted noun + modifier order common in verse). 'بگذارد' is the present/subjunctive or optative form of 'to place' ('he would place / were to place'); in conditional clauses this expresses the hypothetical action. 'اسب' is the direct object 'horse.' Word order (verb before object) is stylistically inverted but yields 'were to place a horse upon that threshold's dust.'
segments.s671-g199: زبهرام is the prepositional phrase 'زِ بهرام' (from/of Bahram) placed up front for emphasis. مه here is used as an adjective in the poetic register ('great, mighty') modifying مغز 'core/brain'; مغز + بادا forms an optative: بادا is the archaic/jussive particle meaning 'may it be' (so مغز بادا = 'may (the) core be'). The second pair مه پوست repeats the same pattern (مه = 'great' modifying پوست = 'skin/outer surface'). Word order is poetic: the optative بادا follows the noun it governs, and repeated مه emphasises both inner (مغز) and outer (پوست) qualities as wished to be 'of Bahram' by the opening زِ بهرام.
segments.s671-g200: نه at the start of the segment is simple negation 'not'. آن کم‌بها را is 'that low‑valued one' with را as the definite/object/emphatic marker; کم‌بها is a compound adjective 'of little value/cheap'. که introduces a relative clause 'which/that' and links to بهرام ازوست: ازوست is an older/contracted form (literally از اوست) meaning 'is of him/belongs to him.' So the whole segment negates and qualifies the preceding wish: 'not that low‑valued thing that belongs to Bahram.'
segments.s671-g177: بیاورد is a past narrative verb 'he brought' (classical Persian past). وبنهاد is the conjunctive form (و + بنهاد) meaning 'and placed/put down.' درپیش is a prepositional compound ('before/in the presence of') and follows the verbs here to indicate the location; شاه is the definite subject/target 'the king.' The clause literally orders: 'he brought and placed (it) before the king.'
segments.s671-g178: همی‌کرد is the periphrastic imperfective (همی + کرد) yielding a continuous past sense 'kept doing' or 'was doing.' شاه is the subject; اندر is the preposition 'in/at,' taking آهن as its object. آهن here is poetic for steel/weapon and نگاه is the noun 'gaze' — the construction means 'the king was gazing at the steel.' Word order is somewhat inverted for poetic effect (verb phrase before the locative).
segments.s671-g179: بفرمود is a polite/honorific past 'he commanded/ordered.' تا introduces a subordinate clause of purpose or command ('that/so that'), so تیغها (the swords) with بشکنند (subjunctive/plural verb) expresses the order: 'that they break the swords' or more idiomatically 'that they break the swords.' The subjunctive بشکنند follows تا in classical Persian to express the commanded action.
segments.s671-g180: دران is the contracted prepositional phrase در + آن 'in/into that.' سله is the place/name of the container and is followed by the adjective نابکار ('accursed'/'wicked'), i.e. سله نابکار 'that accursed scabbard/pack.' افگنند is a plural past-subjunctive/imperative-form verb 'they cast/they threw' matching the earlier ordered agents; together: 'they should cast (them) into that accursed sella.'
segments.s671-g181: فرستاد is a simple past 'he sent/dispatched.' نزدِیک here functions adverbially/locatively ('near/to the presence of'), so 'فرستاد نزدیک بهرام' means 'he sent (him) to Bahram's presence'; باز (literally 'again') marks repetition. Classical Persian often omits an explicit object for 'send' when context supplies it (i.e., 'he sent [a messenger]').
segments.s671-g182: سخنهای is the plural construct 'words/speeches.' پیکار و رزم are synonymous nouns 'battle and combat/war' joined by و; دراز is the adjective 'long' placed after the nouns for poetic inverted word order, giving 'long speeches of battle and combat.' The whole phrase modifies what was sent with the messenger.
segments.s671-g183: بدو is 'unto him/to it' indicating the direction or recipient. نیمه کرده is a perfect participial phrase 'having half-done / having cut in two' (نیمه + کرده). نهاده‌بجای is a fused compound meaning 'placed in its stead/in its place' (نهاده + بجای). Together they describe an action completed and placed as a substitute: 'having split it and set it in its place.'
segments.s671-g184: پراندیشه is an adjectival compound 'full of thought/ pensive.' شد is the past 'became.' مرد is the noun 'the man,' and برگشته رای is a postpositive descriptive phrase: برگشته ('returned') qualifying رای ('counsel/plan'), here indicating the man's mind/counsel had turned back or returned to thought. The clause means 'the man (who had returned) became thoughtful and full of deliberation.'
segments.s671-g33: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. آن زن is the subject 'that woman'. یلان سینه را is the object phrase 'the warriors' chests' with سینه 'chest/bosom' marked by the definite-object marker را; دید is a simple past 'saw'. گفت follows as a second verb with the same subject (she then 'said'), introducing reported speech.
segments.s671-g34: This segment gives the content of her utterance. پرستنده‌ای is used vocatively/accusatively 'O worshipper' (an -ای ending can make an indef. or vocative). راکه‌ای here is a compressed/poetic sequence introducing the exclamation (را + که + ای) and functions to link the vocative to the predicate; خوب جفت is the predicate 'a good/beautiful match' (جفت = 'mate/match'). The whole is an exclamatory address.
segments.s671-g35: برو is the 2nd-person imperative 'go'. تیز functions adverbially 'swift(ly)'. و links this command to the next: 'and'. آن شیر دل را is the object of the verb بگوی: آن 'that' + شیر دل 'lion‑hearted one, brave man' with را marking it as definite object. بگوی is a jussive/imperative form of گفتن 'tell' (classical/poetic form used here).
segments.s671-g36: که is the subordinating particle 'that' introducing the content of what is to be told. ایدر 'here' marks the place. تو را آمدن is a nominalized construction 'your coming' (تو را with آمدن); نیست 'is not/there is no' negates it. روی (literally 'face') is used idiomatically with آمدن to mean 'occasion/right to come' — so the clause says 'that here there is no right/occasion for you to come', i.e. 'you have no business coming here.'
segments.s671-g37: همی‌باش is an imperative/form of counsel 'remain, stay'. نزدیک is an adverb/preposition 'near'. یاران خویش = 'your/his own companions' where یاران is plural 'companions' and خویش+ش is the possessive 'own/his' (classical clitic split here), yielding 'stay near your comrades.'
segments.s671-g38: هم‌اکنون 'immediately/right now'. بیادت ('بیاد') here means 'to the presence of / in the regard of' or idiomatically 'in the matter of' (classical usage: lit. 'in the memory/ regard'). بهرام is the name Bahram. پیش here means 'before / to the presence of' — the phrase commands prompt movement to Bahram's presence.
segments.s671-g39: بدین سان is an idiom 'in this manner / thus'. پیامش is 'his message' or 'the message (to him)' (پیام + possessive suffix ش). ز بهرام is the source 'from Bahram' (ز = from, poetic). ده is the imperative 'give' (classical short form of بده). Word order places the phrase 'پیامش ز بهرام' before the verb, a typical Persian arrangement where the verb comes at the end.
segments.s671-g40: دلش را 'his heart' (دل + possessive ش) marked by را as the object to be affected. به برگشتن 'with respect to returning / about returning' — به + infinitive برگشتن indicates the topic/concern. آرام ده is an imperative 'make calm / soothe' (آرام 'calm' + ده 'give' = 'give calm' → 'calm him'). The whole clause is an instruction to reassure him regarding returning.
segments.s671-g49: نهادند is a third-person plural past tense verb ('they placed/laid out'). خوان is the direct object (a banquet/table) following the verb, and گرد باغ اندرون is an adverbial phrase: گرد (= 'around/about') attaches to باغ ('garden') with اندرون ('inner/inside') qualifying it; word order (verb → object → adverbial) is typical of poetic/older Persian inversion.
segments.s671-g50: خورش ساختند is another simple past clause ('they made/prepared dishes'); ساختند is the transitive past 'they made'. ازگمانی فزون is a qualifying phrase meaning 'more than guessed/expected' (ازگمانی = 'by conjecture/beyond what was thought', فزون = 'abundant/more'), placed after the verb phrase for emphasis.
segments.s671-g51: چونان is an adverb 'thus/so'; خورده شد is a perfect/resultive construction where خورده ('eaten/consumed' or 'worn/broken') plus شد ('became/was') yields 'had been broken/consumed' in context. اسب is the noun 'horse', and گردنگشان is a compound with the possessive suffix شان ('their') meaning 'their neck‑ornament/neck‑covering' (گردنگ = neck‑related item); the whole clause is a circumstantial clause 'when their horses' neck‑ornaments had thus been broken'.
segments.s671-g52: ببردند is past plural 'they carried/took'; the logical subject (the swift ones) is the following nominal پویان, a substantive here meaning 'the moving/quick ones' (attendants). بجای نشان is a locative phrase 'to/in the place of the mark/marker' (بجای = 'to/in the place', نشان = 'mark/sign'), giving the destination of the carrying.
segments.s671-g53: بدان زن چوبرگشت forms a temporal/locative clause: بد (prepositional prefix 'to/at') + ان (demonstrative here) → 'to/at that' with زن 'woman', and چوبرگشت ('on returning') makes the whole phrase 'when/when that woman returned'. بهرام گفت is the reporting clause 'Bahram said' (گفت is the past of 'to say').
segments.s671-g54: که introduces reported content ('that' / 'may it be that'); با تاج تو is a prepositional phrase 'with your crown' (تاج noun followed by the possessor تو). مشتری is the subject 'Jupiter' (the planet/deity), باد is the optative particle 'may it be' (used here to express a wish), and جفت is the predicate meaning 'pair/mate' – together, 'that with your crown Jupiter may be paired.'
segments.s671-g55: بدو is an indirect-object phrase 'to her' (ب + او → 'to her/unto it'); گفت is the reporting verb 'he said' introducing direct speech. پیروزگر باش is the direct imperative 'be victorious' where باش is the imperative of 'to be', and زن is a vocative 'woman' addressing her.
segments.s671-g56: همیشه is an adverb 'always' modifying the phrase شکیبا دل ('patient‑hearted' or 'patient of heart'), where شکیبا is an adjective and دل ('heart') is its noun complement. ورای زن functions here as an address/vocative or directional phrase ('O woman' / 'to the woman'), with ورای an archaic prepositional/vocative element and زن the addressee; the entire phrase is a hortative blessing: 'may your heart always be patient, O woman.'
segments.s671-g89: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so' modifying the verb; گفت is the simple past (3rd sg.) 'said' (here introducing speech); پس is a temporal/sequence adverb 'then'; با + noun (با گرامی دبیر) marks the addressee ('to the honored secretary') — classical/poetic Persian often uses با where modern Persian would use به for 'to'.
segments.s671-g90: که introduces the content of the speech ('that' / 'saying that'); کاری here means 'a matter/affair' (object of the admonition); چنین 'such' qualifies کاری; بر دل (literally 'upon the heart') is an idiomatic locution meaning 'to heart' or 'in one's mind'; آسان مگیر is a negative imperative: مگیر (from گرفتن) in the jussive/imperative negative sense 'do not take', with آسان 'lightly/easily' modifying مگیر ('do not take easily/lightly').
segments.s671-g91: نباید is an impersonal modal negative 'one must not / it must not'; گشاد is an adjective 'open/wide' (here 'to open' or 'become open') that functions as the predicate with نباید, and اندرین ('in this' = archaic form of در این) modifies کارلب ('this affair/occupation' — a compound/poetic form). Together they mean 'this affair ought not to be opened up.'
segments.s671-g92: بر شاه is a prepositional phrase 'upon the king'; باید + شدن forms a necessity construction where شدن (infinitive) follows باید to mean 'must happen / must become'; تیره شب is a noun phrase with adjective before noun 'dark/ominous night'. The clause means 'an ominous/dark night must befall the king.'
segments.s671-g93: چو (here fused in the surface token) is the temporal marker 'when'; the following phrase uses را after the name (بهرام را) as a topical/possessive marker to introduce whose heart is meant; دل پراز تاج گشت is a small clause: دل 'heart' + پراز 'full of' + تاج 'crown' + گشت (past 'became'), meaning 'his heart became filled with the crown.'
segments.s671-g94: همان تخت 'that very throne' is the subject phrase; زیراندرش is a fused locative 'beneath him/under it' (زیر + اندر + -ش) giving the position; عاج گشت is predicate 'became ivory' (عاج 'ivory' + گشت 'became/turned into'). Word order is topic–locative–predicate, typical of poetic Persian.
segments.s671-g95: زدند is simple past 3rd plural 'they struck/did' used idiomatically with رای to mean 'they took/used/offered' (i.e., they tried/put forward); اندران (archaic/inverted اندران) = در این 'in this' modifies کار 'affair'; هرگونه رای 'every kind of counsel/plan' is the object. The clause means 'they tried every kind of counsel in that matter.'
segments.s671-g96: همه چاره 'all remedies/solutions' is the subject; از رفتن 'from going' uses the gerund/infinitive رفتن with the preposition از to indicate source/origin; آمد (past 'came') + بجای (literally 'in place' / 'instead') forms the idiom 'آمد بجای' meaning 'came to be (only) — i.e. it all amounted to departure.' So the whole clause says 'every solution came to leaving.'
segments.s671-g257: کنون is an initial adverb meaning “now.” چاره دام او is a noun phrase meaning “the remedy/solution for his trap” (چاره governs دامِ او, i.e. remedy of his snare). چون here is the interrogative “how,” and کنیم is the 1st‑person plural subjunctive/imperative (“shall we do / let us do”), so the segment asks “Now, how shall we deal with his snare?”
segments.s671-g258: که introduces the subordinate/result/purpose clause (“so that / in order that / that”), and آسان qualifies the manner (“easily”). سر از بند بیرون کردن is a fixed literal phrase (“to get one’s head out of the fetter” = to free oneself from the bond), where سر (head) + از (out of) + بند (fetter/bond) + بیرون (out) form the object phrase; کنیم is again 1st‑person plural subjunctive expressing the desired action (“we may/get out”).
segments.s671-g259: شهنشاه is the subject “the king / emperor.” راکارهاساختست is an archaic fused form meaning “has arranged/made the affairs” (ساختست is the past/perfective form with the classical -ست ending). Word order is subject then verb; the verb denotes a completed action by the Shah.
segments.s671-g260: وزین is the compound of و + زِ این (“and/with this”) used adverbially here (“and by this / with this”). چاره is “remedy,” بی‑رنج means “without toil / easily,” and پرداختست is a past/perfective verb meaning “he accomplished/finished” (classical -ست ending). Together the phrase means that by this remedy he achieved it without effort.
segments.s671-g261: شما هریکی addresses the audience distributively (“each of you”). چاره جان is a noun phrase meaning “a remedy for (one’s) life” (جان is governed by چاره, i.e. remedy of life), and کنید is the 2nd‑person plural present/subjunctive/imperative “do / devise” — an instruction to each addressee.
segments.s671-g262: بدین is the instrumental/locative phrase “by/with this” (here written as ب + دین in the segmentation), and خستگی is the object “fatigue / weariness.” تاچه is the interrogative adverb “to what extent / what (how much),” asking about the degree or kind of remedy, درمان is “cure / remedy,” and کنید is the 2nd‑person plural verb again. The clause asks, essentially, “in view of this weariness, what cure will you make?”
segments.s671-g263: من is the subject “I.” از راز پردخته کردن is a verbal phrase “to speak/relate the secret” with پردخته “well‑wrought / composed” qualifying راز; کردم is the past tense “I did / I have done.” دلم (دل + ـم) begins the next clause and is the possessed noun “my heart.” Note that the segmentation shows the possessive clitic separately (م), but here it is the possessive suffix making دلَم “my heart.”
segments.s671-g264: زتیمارجان is a fused prepositional phrase (ز = از “from / of” + تیمارِ جان “the care of the soul/ life”) meaning roughly “from the care of life.” را is the definite/object marker applied to تیمارجان, and همی‑بگسلم is an older present/future form (همی + بگسلم from گسستن) meaning “I will break off / I will separate (myself).” Grammatically the clause says the speaker’s heart will part/sever itself from the care of life.
segments.s671-g129: همه گفتنیها بدوبازگفت is a compact clause meaning 'he answered all the sayable things.' همه modifies گفتنیها (a nominalized adjective meaning 'things to be said'); بدو باز گفت is an older/poetic fused form meaning 'he replied in return' (the subject 'he/they' is omitted and understood from context).
segments.s671-g130: همه رازها برگشاد از نهفت literally 'all secrets were opened from hiding.' رازها is the direct object/topic, برگشاد is a perfective/past participial verb 'opened/revealed', and از نهفت is an ablative phrase 'out of concealment' (نهفت = 'hidden'). Word order (object — verb — adverbial) is typical of classical Persian.
segments.s671-g131: چنین تا ازان بیشه و مرغزار sets the scene: چنین 'thus/so' + تا 'as far as/even' + ازان 'from that/there' introduces the source/location (بیشه و مرغزار = 'grove and meadow'). The particles appear before the nouns in classical order to indicate manner and source.
segments.s671-g132: یکایک همی‌گفت با شهریار: یکایک 'one by one' modifies the (omitted) subjects, همی‌گفت is the habitual/imperfective form 'kept saying / would say,' and با شهریار ('with the king') here functions as the addressee ('to the king') in classical usage rather than strictly 'with.'
segments.s671-g133: وزان رفتن گور و آن راه تنگ is a list-starting phrase: وزان 'and from that' links to previous events; رفتن گور is a verbal-noun + noun sequence 'going to the grave' (i.e. death or burial) where رفتن functions as a nominalized verb and گور = 'grave'; آن راه تنگ is a separate item 'that narrow path.'
segments.s671-g134: ز آرام بهرام و چندین درنگ continues the list: ز is the poetic form of از 'from,' آرام here denotes 'rest/calm' associated with بهرام (Bahram), and چندین درنگ means 'several pauses' or 'many delays.' The whole phrase enumerates further occurrences following the previous item.
segments.s671-g135: وزان رفتن کاخ گوهرنگار: وزان 'and from that' again links items; رفتن here functions as a nominalized event 'the departure' and کاخ گوهرنگار is the place 'the jewel‑adorned palace.' Together they indicate 'the leaving of the jeweled palace.'
segments.s671-g136: پرستندگان و زن تاجدار names who left: پرستندگان 'the worshippers' and زن تاجدار 'the crown‑wearing woman' (تاجدار postpositively qualifying زن). Grammatically this is the (omitted) subject of the nominalized رفتن in the previous segment.
segments.s671-g337: دبیر بزرگ آن زمان لب ببست is an S‑(modifiers) + O + V sequence typical of Persian poetry. دبیر بزرگ آن زمان is a noun phrase 'the great/ chief scribe at that time' (آن زمان = 'at that time' adverbially). لب is the (direct) object 'lip(s)' placed before the verb, and ببست is the past tense 3rd singular 'he closed/shut' (simple past). The possessor ('his') of لب is understood from context and omitted.
segments.s671-g338: بانبوه اندیشه اندر نشست is an adverbial/nominal phrase + verb: بانبوه ('in great numbers' / 'a crowd of') modifies اندیشه ('thoughts'), then اندر نشست = 'sat in/within' or more idiomatically 'sat down in (them)'. نشست is used as a past action 'he sat' (3rd singular). The whole clause means he sat/was seated immersed in many thoughts (an idiomatic way to say 'fell into deep thought').
segments.s671-g339: ازان پس چنین گفت بهرام را combines a temporal phrase and a speech verb: ازان پس ('after that' / 'thereupon') + چنین گفت ('thus said'). بهرام را marks the addressee/definite object with را; in classical/poetic Persian را can appear after the name of the person addressed to indicate definite/emphatic object or dative sense ('to Bahram'). گفت here is the speech verb 'said' (past 3rd sing.).
segments.s671-g340: که هرکس که جویا بود کامرا is a subordinate (reported) clause introduced by که after گفت. هرکس که ... is the idiomatic 'whoever that ...' construction: هرکس (whoever) + که (relative particle) + جویا بود ('was seeking' – جویا is a predicative adjective/participle and بود is the past copula). کامرا (= کام را, fused) is the object 'desire/wish' placed after the predicate. The clause literally: 'that whoever was seeking desire...'.
segments.s671-g341: چودرخور بجوید بیابد همان: چودرخور is a fused adverbial 'چو درخور' meaning 'as befits' / 'worthily'. بجوید is a 3rd‑person-form of the verb 'seek' (here general/prescriptive: 'seeks' or 'should seek'), and بیابد is a 3rd‑person optative/indicative 'finds'/'will find' (classical Persian frequently uses this form to express general truths). همان ('the same' / 'that very thing') refers back to کامرا in the previous clause. The order mirrors poetic inversion but reads: 'Whoever worthily seeks will find that (same thing).'
segments.s671-g342: درازست ویازنده دست زمان is an elliptical predicate clause. درازست = 'it is long' (here 'long in reach/time'); ویازنده ('the offender' / 'one who harms') modifies the following noun phrase. دست زمان = 'the hand (power) of Time' (a possessive/genitive grouping: دستِ زمان). Put together: 'the hand of Time is long for the offender' — i.e. Time has a long arm to punish the wrongdoer. Note the poetic inversion: adjective/predicate before the noun phrase it characterizes.
segments.s671-g343: زچیزی که بخشش کند دادگر is a relative phrase with poetic inversion. زچیزی که = 'from/whatever thing that', بخشش کند = 'gives/grants' (بخشش as 'a grant/gift' + کند 'does/gives'), and دادگر ('the just one' / 'just ruler') stands as the agent subject placed after the verb for emphasis (inversion common in epic style). Literally: 'of that which the just one grants'.
segments.s671-g344: چنان دان که کوشش بیاید ببر begins with an imperative/jussive: چنان دان = 'consider (it) so' or 'know it thus' (دان = consider/know). که introduces the explanatory clause: 'that/so that': کوشش ('effort') بیاید ('may come' / 'arrives' — here expressing that endeavour yields results) and ببر is an imperative 'take!/seize!' (addressing the addressee to accept whatever their own effort brings). The sequence gives a rule: regard a just ruler's grants as given, and when effort brings them, take them.
segments.s671-g9: This is a prepositional/adverbial phrase: 'پیاده' (on foot) modifies the verb that follows. 'ز' is the poetic preposition 'from'. The noun chain 'دهلیز کاخ اندرون' is a genitive/compound sequence (corridor of the palace's inner part) read right-to-left: 'the corridor of the inner palace.'
segments.s671-g10: 'همی‌رفت' is the classical/poetic imperfective past form (like می‌رفت) meaning 'was going' or 'went on/was walking.' The proper name 'بهرام' is the subject, and 'بی‌رهنمون' is a postpositive adjective/phrase meaning 'without a guide' that qualifies him. Word order (verb before subject) and the use of همی‑ emphasize continuous action in classical style.
segments.s671-g11: 'زمانی' is a temporal adverb meaning 'at that time/once.' The small clause 'بدر بود' is a predicate 'the moon was full' with 'بود' as the past copula. 'ایزد گشسب' immediately follows as the actor: here a compound/epithet (Izad‑Gushasp), the heroic name that will be the subject of the next verbal phrase.
segments.s671-g12: 'گرفته' is a past participle/predicate 'having taken/seized' placed before its complement; 'بدست' here means 'in (his) hand' (i.e. 'holding'). 'آن گرانمایه اسب' is the definite noun phrase 'that precious/noble steed' which is the thing taken. Together the phrase describes Gushasp holding or having seized the noble horse.
segments.s671-g13: 'یلان' (plural) means 'the heroes' and is the grammatical subject; the idiom 'سینه آمد' literally 'came to the breast' means they pressed close or crowded in upon him. 'پس' is the connective 'then/after that,' and 'او دوان' places the pronoun 'he' with the verb 'دوان' (ran/rushed) — classical diction where verb forms can appear without an explicit auxiliary.
segments.s671-g14: 'براسب' is a prepositional/adverbial compound meaning 'onto/on the horse.' 'تگاور' here functions as a name or agent (Tegavar — cf. earlier context) placed next to the verb. 'ببسته' is a past form meaning 'had bound/fastened,' and 'میان' ('between'/'amid') indicates placement; together the clause says that Tegavar had fastened/bound him onto the horse (i.e. secured him on the horse's back). Word order follows classical epic patterns (agent and modifiers arranged around the verb).
segments.s671-g15: 'بدو گفت' is the common classical order meaning 'he said to him' (بدو = to him; گفت = said). 'ایزد گشسب دلیر' is the speaker's name plus epithet: 'Izad‑Gushasp, the brave' — the proper name appears after the verb (inverted order typical of epic poetry) and 'دلیر' is an attributive adjective 'brave.'
segments.s671-g16: This is the vocative/exclamatory address. 'که‌ای' (classical) here functions as a vocative particle 'O' addressed to someone; 'پرهنر' and 'نامبرد' are stacked epithets 'skilled/cultured' and 'renowned/the named one' that modify 'شیر' (literally 'lion' but used metaphorically as 'hero'/'brave man'). The sequence is attributive adjectives + epithet + metaphorical noun of address.
segments.s671-g281: ز (preposition, poetic) + ایران marks provenance/association: 'of/from Iran'. سرانید is a 2nd-person plural/copular form here meaning 'you are (the) chiefs/leaders' (poetic word order: predicate with person ending). وجنگ‌آوران begins with the coordinating و 'and' attached to جنگ‌آوران 'warriors', continuing the appositive list.
segments.s671-g282: A string of adjectives/nouns describing those addressed: خردمند 'wise' modifies the addressees; ودانا (with initial و = 'and') adds 'knowing/learned'; وافسونگران (و + افسونگران) adds 'and enchanters/sorcerers'. The coordinating و attaches each descriptor to the preceding noun phrase.
segments.s671-g283: چه is an interrogative ('why/what'); بینید is 2nd-person plural present (or hortative/interrogative) 'do you see/look'; یکسر is an adverb 'entirely/wholly' modifying the verb phrase; به کار اندرون is a prepositional phrase 'to/onto the affairs within' (به + noun + اندرون 'inner matters'). Word order places the interrogative first, then verb, then adverb and prepositional complement.
segments.s671-g284: Another interrogative clause: چه 'what' + بازی 'play/game, trick' as the object; نهید here is the verb 'to place/set/do' (2nd-person plural poetic form 'do you set/are you playing'); اندرین 'in these/this' specifies location; دشت خون 'plain of blood' is a genitive compound (دشت + خون). The clause asks 'what game are you setting/playing in this blood-strewn plain?'
segments.s671-g285: چنین 'thus' is an adverb introducing reported speech. گفت is the past 3rd-person 'said' (here used before the speaker's name, a common poetic word order). ایزد گشسب سوار is the nominative/title following the verb: 'the god Gushasp, the rider' (noun phrase with apposition).
segments.s671-g286: This is the beginning of the quoted address. که‌ای functions as an interrogative/vocative 'who (are you), O ...' (an archaic/poetic combination of که 'who/that' with the vocative/particle ای). ازگرانمایگان is a genitive phrase 'of the noble/dignified ones' (from the nobles); یادگار is the predicate/vocative 'remnant/memento' applied to the addressee. The whole is 'Who are you, O remnant of the nobles?'
segments.s671-g287: زبانهای 'tongues/speech(s)' (subject). ماگر here is an archaic conditional/exceptional particle ('if/if it should be that' / 'unless'); شود is 3rd-person singular present/subjunctive 'becomes/are (be)'; تیغ نیز 'the sword also' (نیز = 'also') begins the result clause. The clause as a whole sets a conditional: 'If our tongues are (made) silent, the sword also ...'.
segments.s671-g288: زدریای should be read as زِ دریای 'from the sea (of...)' (preposition ز + دریای). رای تو 'your counsel/opinion' is a genitive phrase (دریای رایِ تو = 'the sea of your counsel'). گیرد گریز is a verbal phrase: گیرد (3rd-sg present/simple future 'takes/seizes') + گریز 'flight/escape' = 'takes flight'. So: 'the sword also will, from the sea of your counsel, take flight.'
segments.s671-g385: بسی بد که introduces a judgemental clause: بسی (intensifier 'very/much') + بد ('bad') + که ('that') begins the subordinate clause. بیکار بدتخت شاه is a noun phrase with two prepositive adjectives (بیکار 'idle', بدتخت 'ill‑fated') modifying شاه ('the king').
segments.s671-g386: نکرد is the negated past verb 'did not do' with its subject (the king) carried over from the previous segment. اندرو here means 'at/in it' or 'thereon' (referring back to something in the context). هیچ‌کهتر is an emphatic negative ('not even the least / no smaller [glance]') and نگاه is the object 'a glance/look' that the verb negates.
segments.s671-g387: جهان را is the definite object ('the world' + object marker را). بمردی is an adverbial phrase 'with manliness / in a manly way' modifying the verbal compound نگه داشتند, where نگه (root 'keeping/watch') + داشتند (past 'they held/kept') together mean 'they maintained / kept (in being)'.
segments.s671-g388: یکی is an indefinite 'someone / one person'. چشم here is used literally 'eye' (metonymically 'a watcher'), برتخت 'on the throne' locates the object, and نگماشتند (from گماشت) means 'they set/appointed' — together: 'they appointed someone to watch the throne.'
segments.s671-g389: هرآنکس که is the relative 'whoever'. دانا is the predicate adjective 'wise'. بدو ('to him') indicates relation or orientation toward someone mentioned earlier (i.e., 'toward him'). پاک مغز is a compound adjective phrase 'pure‑minded / clean of mind' that qualifies that person.
segments.s671-g390: زهرگونه is a modifier 'of poisonous kind / of every poisonous sort' qualifying اندیشه‌ای ('a thought / any thought'). راند is the past verb 'drove away / expelled'. نغز is an adverbial qualifier meaning 'aptly / pithily / cleverly', characterizing how the thoughts were driven off.
segments.s671-g391: بداند is a 3rd‑person present/subjunctive form meaning 'he should know' or 'let him know'. که introduces the content clause. شاهی ('kingship') is the subject and به ... است is the predication: به + ازبندگیست expresses that kingship is founded on or comes through 'ازبندگی' (servitude/devotion); note the fused poetic copula -است.
segments.s671-g392: همان ('that very') picks up and emphasizes what follows. سرافرازی is 'pride / glory / exaltation' as the subject, and زافگندگیست is a compound predicate meaning 'is from being cast down / is the consequence of ruin' — a moral observation that such pride leads to downfall.
segments.s671-g81: نشستی here functions as a noun meaning 'a seat/sitting' (classical Persian often uses nouns where modern texts might use a compound). It is followed by the qualifier بیاراست, a post‑nominal adjective/participle 'well‑adorned' that modifies the noun. شاهنشهی is another post‑nominal modifier in the genitive/attributive sense 'imperial' (literally 'of kingship'), so the whole phrase reads 'a well‑adorned imperial seat.'
segments.s671-g82: نهاده is a past participle/verb 'placed' (passive/causative sense), and the following prepositional pieces specify location: به سر بر = 'upon the head' (به + سر with the emphatic/locative بر 'on/upon'). کلاه مهی is the object placed: کلاه 'cap/hat' qualified by مهی 'moon‑like, shining (beautiful)'. The word order is verb/participle + locative phrase + noun phrase describing what was placed.
segments.s671-g83: نگه کرد is a light verb construction: نگه (watch/keeping) + کرد 'did/made' = 'kept watch' or 'watched'. کارش 'his affair/work' is the object (کار + enclitic ش 'his'), and دبیر بزرگ 'the great scribe' is the agent/subject; word order places verb and object before the subject for emphasis (common in poetry).
segments.s671-g84: بدانست is 'he perceived/knew it' (perfective past). کو here functions as an interrogative/relative particle meaning 'who' (classical usage), introducing the person identified; شد is the past of 'to become', and دلیر و سترگ are predicates 'brave and [grand/majestic]' describing that person. The clause therefore means 'he knew who had become brave and majestic.'
segments.s671-g85: چو 'when' introduces a circumstantial clause. نزدیک 'near' modifies the verb رسید 'arrived/reached'; خراد برزین is the proper name (Kharad Barzin) performing the arrival. The order is standard: temporal conjunction + locative/adverb + name + verb 'arrived.'
segments.s671-g86: بگفت is 'he said' (simple past), and آنچ دانست و دید و شنید is a coordinated object: آنچ 'that which' + three verbs in the past tense or perfective (دانست 'knew', دید 'saw', شنید 'heard'), meaning 'he told that which he knew and had seen and heard.' The sequence lists the sources of his report (knowing, seeing, hearing).
segments.s671-g87: چو 'when' introduces the clause. خراد برزین (the name) is the subject and شنید 'heard' is the verb; این سخن 'these words/this speech' is the object. Word order is Subject + Verb + Object (a common poetic order), producing 'When Kharad Barzin heard these words...'.
segments.s671-g88: بدانست 'he knew/realized' introduces a subordinate content clause. کان here is an archaic particle meaning roughly 'that/those' (classical usage), رنجها 'the hardships/sufferings' is the subject, شد 'became' is the verbal predicate, and کهن 'old/ancient' is the resulting state: 'he realized that those hardships had grown old.'
segments.s671-g57: This is an archaic temporel/clausal phrase: چو (here written fused with بهرام) = “when,” followed by the subject بهرام. زان کاخ is a prepositional phrase meaning “from that palace” (زان = “from that/thereupon,” کاخ = “palace”). آمد برون is the verb phrase, آمد (past of آمدن) “came” + برون “out/forth.” Word order is ‘temporal conjunction + subject + source + verb + directional adverb.’
segments.s671-g58: تو گفتی is literally “you said” (تو = second-person singular).
